---
title: "Sourcing Playbook and Framework Agreements"
description: "Source note on applying Sourcing Playbook policies when using framework agreements."
---

## Source

This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on using the Sourcing Playbook in conjunction with framework agreements [@cabinet_office_gcf_2023_sourcing_framework_agreements].

The main source is:

- [GOV.UK HTML: Using the Playbook in Conjunction with Framework Agreements](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/the-sourcing-and-consultancy-playbooks/using-the-playbook-in-conjunction-with-framework-agreements-html)

## One-Page Summary

This guidance clarifies how Sourcing Playbook policy responsibilities are shared between framework providers and calling-off contracting authorities. The central message for VfM rubric creation is that use of a framework does not remove the need for proportionate sourcing discipline.

The source distinguishes framework provider responsibilities, calling-off authority responsibilities, and enabling information that a framework provider can make available. For example, a framework provider may carry out market health assessment before establishing the framework, while a calling-off authority may still need to consider whether the framework is the right route for its specific requirement.

## Purpose and Scope

The guidance applies where departments use framework agreements to meet service or capability needs. It is most relevant to rubric creation where a VfM judgement might otherwise assume that framework use is automatically economical, efficient, or compliant with the Sourcing Playbook.

## VfM-Relevant Concepts

Direct source claims:

- Framework providers and calling-off authorities can hold different parts of the evidence base.
- Market health and capability assessment may be undertaken at framework level, but call-off authorities may need supplementary assessment for their own requirements.
- Framework use should still be considered against the delivery model, market, risk, and value questions raised by the Sourcing Playbook.

Inferred VfM implications:

- A VfM rubric should test the fit between the framework and the specific requirement, not just whether a framework route was available.
- Frameworks can reduce transaction cost, but they can also limit competition, lock out new suppliers, or weaken requirement-specific evidence if used uncritically.
- Evidence ownership matters: some rubric evidence will sit with the framework provider and some with the call-off authority.

## Implications for Rubric Criteria

| VfM criterion | Framework-specific rubric implication |
|---|---|
| Economy | Test whether framework pricing and competition remain appropriate for the requirement. |
| Efficiency | Test whether framework use reduced avoidable process cost without skipping essential analysis. |
| Effectiveness | Test whether the framework can deliver the specific service outcomes and quality thresholds. |
| Equity and fairness | Test whether supplier access, competition, and transparency are preserved at framework and call-off stages. |
| Risk and resilience | Test whether framework-level risk controls are sufficient for the criticality of the call-off. |

## Evidence Useful for Rubric Creation

Candidate evidence:

- framework provider customer guidance;
- framework-level market health and capability assessment;
- call-off market and delivery model assessment;
- rationale for using the framework route;
- call-off competition records and evaluation report;
- framework pricing information and benchmark evidence;
- criticality assessment for the call-off;
- supplier economic and financial standing checks where relevant.

## Relevance Rules Applied

Include framework material where it clarifies accountability, evidence ownership, market effects, supplier access, price discipline, or delivery model fit.

Use framework procedural detail only as supporting context unless it changes the VfM judgement.

## Limits and Exclusions

This note does not treat framework compliance as a VfM standard by itself. The relevant VfM question is whether the framework route was suitable and well used for the specific requirement.

## Links

See also the [Sourcing Playbook](sourcing-playbook.qmd), [Market Management](sourcing-market-management.qmd), and [Supplier Financial Standing](sourcing-supplier-financial-standing.qmd) notes.

Return to the [notes overview](index.qmd).

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
